summary: Acetylcholine is the neurotransmitter at the neuromuscular junction, binding to nicotinic receptors on the motor end plate.
Noradrenaline is the neurotransmitter at the neuromuscular junction.
Acetylcholine binds to muscarinic receptors at the motor end plate.
Acetylcholinesterase rapidly hydrolyses acetylcholine in the synaptic cleft to terminate neuromuscular transmission.
Monoamine oxidase is responsible for degrading acetylcholine at the NMJ.
Acetylcholine is removed from the cleft by active reuptake into the nerve terminal.
In excitation-contraction coupling, calcium binds to troponin C, causing tropomyosin to move and expose actin binding sites for myosin cross-bridge formation.
Calcium binds directly to myosin to initiate cross-bridge cycling.
Tropomyosin binds calcium to expose the myosin binding sites on actin.
Saltatory conduction in myelinated fibres is faster because action potentials jump between nodes of Ranvier, skipping myelinated segments.
Myelinated fibres conduct by continuous conduction along the entire axon length.
Nodes of Ranvier are the myelinated segments where conduction is fastest.
The all-or-none law states that once threshold is reached, the action potential fires at full amplitude regardless of stimulus strength.
Increasing stimulus strength above threshold increases the amplitude of the action potential.
Subthreshold stimuli can generate small action potentials.
During the absolute refractory period, no stimulus of any strength can generate a second action potential because sodium channels are inactivated.
During the absolute refractory period, a very strong stimulus can still generate an action potential.
The refractory period occurs before the action potential, not after it.
The resting membrane potential of a neuron is approximately -70 mV, maintained primarily by the sodium-potassium ATPase pump.
The resting membrane potential is approximately +40 mV.
The resting potential is maintained by chloride channels alone.
